<member name="T:ModelContextProtocol.Server.McpServerToolAttribute">
            <summary>
            Indicates that a method should be considered an <see cref="T:ModelContextProtocol.Server.McpServerTool"/>.
            </summary>
            <remarks>
            <para>
            This attribute is applied to methods that should be exposed as tools in the Model Context Protocol. When a class
            containing methods marked with this attribute is registered with McpServerBuilderExtensions,
            these methods become available as tools that can be called by MCP clients.
            </para>
            <para>
            When methods are provided directly to <see cref="M:McpServerTool.Create"/>, the attribute is not required.
            </para>
            <para>
            By default, parameters are sourced from the <see cref="P:ModelContextProtocol.Protocol.CallToolRequestParams.Arguments"/> dictionary, which is a collection
            of key/value pairs, and are represented in the JSON schema for the function, as exposed in the returned <see cref="T:ModelContextProtocol.Server.McpServerTool"/>'s
            <see cref="P:ModelContextProtocol.Server.McpServerTool.ProtocolTool"/>'s <see cref="P:ModelContextProtocol.Protocol.Tool.InputSchema"/>. Those parameters are deserialized from the
            <see cref="T:System.Text.Json.JsonElement"/> values in that collection. There are a few exceptions to this:
            <list type="bullet">
              <item>
                <description>
                  <see cref="T:System.Threading.CancellationToken"/> parameters are automatically bound to a <see cref="T:System.Threading.CancellationToken"/> provided by the
                  <see cref="T:ModelContextProtocol.Server.McpServer"/> and that respects any <see cref="T:ModelContextProtocol.Protocol.CancelledNotificationParams"/>s sent by the client for this operation's
                  <see cref="T:ModelContextProtocol.Protocol.RequestId"/>. The parameter is not included in the generated JSON schema.
                </description>
              </item>
              <item>
                <description>
                  <see cref="T:System.IServiceProvider"/> parameters are bound from the <see cref="T:ModelContextProtocol.Server.RequestContext`1"/> for this request,
                  and are not included in the JSON schema.
                </description>
              </item>
              <item>
                <description>
                  <see cref="T:ModelContextProtocol.Server.McpServer"/> parameters are not included in the JSON schema and are bound directly to the <see cref="T:ModelContextProtocol.Server.McpServer"/>
                  instance associated with this request's <see cref="T:ModelContextProtocol.Server.RequestContext`1"/>. Such parameters may be used to understand
                  what server is being used to process the request, and to interact with the client issuing the request to that server.
                </description>
              </item>
              <item>
                <description>
                  <see cref="T:System.IProgress`1"/> parameters accepting <see cref="T:ModelContextProtocol.ProgressNotificationValue"/> values
                  are not included in the JSON schema and are bound to an <see cref="T:System.IProgress`1"/> instance manufactured
                  to forward progress notifications from the tool to the client. If the client included a <see cref="T:ModelContextProtocol.Protocol.ProgressToken"/> in their request,
                  progress reports issued to this instance will propagate to the client as <see cref="F:ModelContextProtocol.Protocol.NotificationMethods.ProgressNotification"/> notifications with
                  that token. If the client did not include a <see cref="T:ModelContextProtocol.Protocol.ProgressToken"/>, the instance will ignore any progress reports issued to it.
                </description>
              </item>
              <item>
                <description>
                  When the <see cref="T:ModelContextProtocol.Server.McpServerTool"/> is constructed, it may be passed an <see cref="T:System.IServiceProvider"/> via
                  <see cref="P:ModelContextProtocol.Server.McpServerToolCreateOptions.Services"/>. Any parameter that can be satisfied by that <see cref="T:System.IServiceProvider"/>
                  according to <see cref="T:Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection.IServiceProviderIsService"/> will not be included in the generated JSON schema and will be resolved
                  from the <see cref="T:System.IServiceProvider"/> provided when the tool is invoked rather than from the argument collection.
                </description>
              </item>
              <item>
                <description>
                  Any parameter attributed with <see cref="T:Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection.FromKeyedServicesAttribute"/> will similarly be resolved from the
                  <see cref="T:System.IServiceProvider"/> provided when the tool is invoked rather than from the argument
                  collection, and will not be included in the generated JSON schema.
                </description>
              </item>
            </list>
            </para>

1. # MCP C# SDK
2. 
3. [![NuGet version](https://img.shields.io/nuget/v/ModelContextProtocol.svg)](https://www.nuget.org/packages/ModelContextProtocol)
4. 
5. The official C# SDK for the [Model Context Protocol](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/), enabling .NET applications, services, and libraries to implement and interact with MCP clients and servers. Please visit the [API documentation](https://csharp.sdk.modelcontextprotocol.io/api/ModelContextProtocol.html) for more details on available functionality.
6. 
7. ## Packages
8. 
9. This SDK consists of three main packages:

12.

References `ModelContextProtocol.Core`. This is the right fit for most projects that don't need HTTP server capabilities.

References `ModelContextProtocol`.

 # MCP C# SDK

 The official C# SDK for the [Model Context Protocol](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/), enabling .NET applications, services, and libraries to implement and interact with MCP clients and servers. Please visit the [API documentation](https://csharp.sdk.modelcontextprotocol.io/api/ModelContextProtocol.html) for more details on available functionality.
 
 ## Packages
 
 This SDK consists of three main packages:
 
 - **[ModelContextProtocol.Core](https://www.nuget.org/packages/ModelContextProtocol.Core)** [![NuGet version](https://img.shields.io/nuget/v/ModelContextProtocol.Core.svg)](https://www.nuget.org/packages/ModelContextProtocol.Core) - For projects that only need to use the client or low-level server APIs and want the minimum number of dependencies.

References `ModelContextProtocol`.

 ## About MCP
 
 The Model Context Protocol (MCP) is an open protocol that standardizes how applications provide context to Large Language Models (LLMs). It enables secure integration between LLMs and various data sources and tools.
 
 For more information about MCP:
 
 - [Official MCP Documentation](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/)
 - [MCP C# SDK Documentation](https://csharp.sdk.modelcontextprotocol.io/)
 - [Protocol Specification](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/specification/)
 - [GitHub Organization](https://github.com/modelcontextprotocol)

            <summary>

            </summary>

            <para>

            </para>

            when the parent process disconnects.

 # MCP C# SDK

Perfect! Now I have enough information. Let me compile a comprehensive guide based on all the documentation I've found.

## Complete Guide to Building a Stdio MCP Server with ModelContextProtocol 1.2.0

Based on the XML documentation from the installed NuGet packages, here's the complete technical reference:

---

### **1. Building a Stdio MCP Server with IHostBuilder/McpServerBuilder**

**Key Extension Method:**
```csharp
// Namespace: Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection
services.AddMcpServer(options => { /* configure */ })
    .WithStdioServerTransport()
```

**Method Signature:**
- **AddMcpServer**: `IServiceCollection.AddMcpServer(IServiceCollection, Action<McpServerOptions>)` → Returns `IMcpServerBuilder`
- **WithStdioServerTransport**: `IMcpServerBuilder.WithStdioServerTransport(IMcpServerBuilder)` → Returns `IMcpServerBuilder`

**Key Classes:**
- `Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection.IMcpServerBuilder` - fluent builder interface
- `Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection.DefaultMcpServerBuilder` - default implementation
- `ModelContextProtocol.SingleSessionMcpServerHostedService` - hosted service for stdio/single-session servers

**Usage Pattern:**
```csharp
var host = Host.CreateDefaultBuilder(args)
    .ConfigureServices(services =>
    {
        services.AddMcpServer()
            .WithStdioServerTransport()
            .WithTools<MyToolClass>()
            .WithPrompts<MyPromptClass>();
    })
    .Build();

await host.RunAsync();
```

---

### **2. Registering Tools Using Attributes**

**Attributes Required:**
- **`ModelContextProtocol.Server.McpServerToolAttribute`** - Mark methods that should be exposed as tools
- **`ModelContextProtocol.Server.McpServerToolTypeAttribute`** - Mark classes that contain tool methods

**Namespace:** `ModelContextProtocol.Server`

**Example:**
```csharp
[McpServerToolType]
public class MyTools
{
    [McpServerTool(name: "tool_name", description: "Tool description")]
    public string MyTool(string param1, int param2)
    {
        return $"Result: {param1} {param2}";
    }
}
```

**Registration Methods:**
- `IMcpServerBuilder.WithTools<TToolType>(JsonSerializerOptions)` - Auto-discover methods with `[McpServerTool]`
- `IMcpServerBuilder.WithTools<TToolType>(TToolType instance, JsonSerializerOptions)` - Use specific instance
- `IMcpServerBuilder.WithTools(IEnumerable<Type>, JsonSerializerOptions)` - Multiple tool types
- `IMcpServerBuilder.WithToolsFromAssembly(Assembly, JsonSerializerOptions)` - Auto-discover from assembly (scans for `[McpServerToolType]`)

---

### **3. Registering Prompts Using Attributes**

**Attributes Required:**
- **`ModelContextProtocol.Server.McpServerPromptAttribute`** - Mark methods that should be exposed as prompts
- **`ModelContextProtocol.Server.McpServerPromptTypeAttribute`** - Mark classes that contain prompt methods

**Namespace:** `ModelContextProtocol.Server`

**Example:**
```csharp
[McpServerPromptType]
public class MyPrompts
{
    [McpServerPrompt(name: "prompt_name", description: "Prompt description")]
    public string GetPrompt(string argument)
    {
        return "Prompt content";
    }
}
```

**Registration Methods:**
- `IMcpServerBuilder.WithPrompts<TPromptType>(JsonSerializerOptions)` - Auto-discover methods
- `IMcpServerBuilder.WithPrompts<TPromptType>(TPromptType instance, JsonSerializerOptions)` - Use instance
- `IMcpServerBuilder.WithPrompts(IEnumerable<Type>, JsonSerializerOptions)` - Multiple types
- `IMcpServerBuilder.WithPromptsFromAssembly(Assembly, JsonSerializerOptions)` - Auto-discover from assembly

---

### **4. Tool and Prompt Parameter Declaration**

**Parameter Binding Rules** (from `McpServerToolAttribute` documentation):

Parameters are sourced from `CallToolRequestParams.Arguments` dictionary (key-value pairs), BUT with automatic exceptions:

| Parameter Type | Behavior | In Schema? |
|---|---|---|
| `System.Threading.CancellationToken` | Auto-bound to request cancellation token | ❌ No |
| `System.IServiceProvider` | Bound from `RequestContext<T>` | ❌ No |
| `ModelContextProtocol.Server.McpServer` | Bound to the active server instance | ❌ No |
| `System.IProgress<ModelContextProtocol.ProgressNotificationValue>` | Bound to progress notification forwarder | ❌ No |
| **Services from DI** (via `IServiceProviderIsService`) | Resolved from tool's `McpServerToolCreateOptions.Services` | ❌ No |
| **`[FromKeyedServices]` parameters** | Resolved from DI provider | ❌ No |
| **All other parameters** | Deserialized from Arguments dictionary | ✅ Yes |

**Attribute for Parameter Constraints:**
- `System.ComponentModel.DataAnnotations.RequiredAttribute`
- `System.ComponentModel.DataAnnotations.MaxLengthAttribute`
- `System.ComponentModel.DataAnnotations.AllowedValuesAttribute`

These influence the generated JSON schema.

**Example with DI:**
```csharp
[McpServerTool]
public string MyTool(
    string userInput,                    // from Arguments
    HttpClient httpClient,               // from DI (IServiceProviderIsService)
    CancellationToken ct)                // auto-bound
{
    // httpClient is automatically resolved if registered in DI
}
```

---

### **5. Injecting HttpClient via Dependency Injection**

**How It Works:**

1. **Register HttpClient in DI:**
```csharp
services.AddHttpClient(); // or services.AddHttpClient<MyClass>();
```

2. **Configure McpServerTool with Services:**
```csharp
var options = new McpServerToolCreateOptions
{
    Services = sp  // IServiceProvider containing HttpClient
};
```

3. **Automatic Resolution:**
When a tool/prompt method declares an `HttpClient` parameter, the framework checks `IServiceProviderIsService` to determine if it can be satisfied from DI. If yes, it's NOT included in the JSON schema and is injected at invocation time.

**Key Property:**
- `ModelContextProtocol.Server.McpServerToolCreateOptions.Services` - Optional `IServiceProvider` for DI resolution
- `ModelContextProtocol.Server.McpServerPromptCreateOptions.Services` - Same for prompts

**Advanced Pattern:**
```csharp
[FromKeyedServices("namedClient")]  // requires Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection
public HttpClient MyNamedClient { get; set; }
```

---

### **6. Prompt Return Types**

From `McpServerPromptAttribute` documentation:

| Return Type | Behavior |
|---|---|
| `string` | Wrapped in `PromptMessage` in `GetPromptResult.Messages` |
| `ModelContextProtocol.Protocol.PromptMessage` | Single message in result |
| `IEnumerable<PromptMessage>` | Multiple messages in result |
| `Microsoft.Extensions.AI.ChatMessage` | Converted to `PromptMessage` using `ToPromptMessages()` |
| `IEnumerable<ChatMessage>` | Multiple converted messages |

---

### **7. Configuring Logging (NOT to stdout)**

**Critical Issue:** Stdio transport uses stdout for JSON-RPC messages. Do NOT log to stdout.

**Configuration Pattern:**

```csharp
.ConfigureLogging(logging =>
{
    // Disable console logging (which writes to stdout)
    logging.ClearProviders();
    
    // Use alternative: File, EventLog, etc.
    logging.AddFile("logs/mcp-server.log");
    // OR use the built-in ClientLoggerProvider to send logs to client
})
```

**Logging to Client (Optional):**

The MCP protocol supports sending log messages to the client:
- **`ModelContextProtocol.Server.McpServer.ClientLoggerProvider`** - `ILoggerProvider` that sends logs to client

```csharp
logging.AddProvider(new McpServer.ClientLoggerProvider(mcpServer));
```

**Key Caveat:**
- Do NOT use `AddConsole()` with stdio transport
- The framework has `ILoggerFactory` parameter in `McpServer.Create()` - pass a properly configured factory

---

### **8. Key Namespaces and Classes**

| Component | Namespace |
|---|---|
| Extension methods for DI | `Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection` |
| Tool/Prompt attributes | `ModelContextProtocol.Server` |
| Tool/Prompt base classes | `ModelContextProtocol.Server` |
| Server creation | `ModelContextProtocol.Server.McpServer` |
| Stdio transport | `ModelContextProtocol.Server` / `StdioServerTransport` |
| Hosted service | `ModelContextProtocol` / `SingleSessionMcpServerHostedService` |

---

### **9. Full Wiring Example**

```csharp
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;
using Microsoft.Extensions.Hosting;
using Microsoft.Extensions.Logging;
using ModelContextProtocol.Server;

[McpServerToolType]
public class MyTools
{
    private readonly HttpClient _httpClient;

    [McpServerTool(name: "fetch_data", description: "Fetch data from URL")]
    public async Task<string> FetchData(
        string url,
        HttpClient httpClient,  // Injected from DI
        CancellationToken ct = default)
    {
        var response = await httpClient.GetAsync(url, ct);
        return await response.Content.ReadAsStringAsync();
    }
}

[McpServerPromptType]
public class MyPrompts
{
    [McpServerPrompt(name: "summary")]
    public string GetSummaryPrompt(string context)
    {
        return $"Summarize: {context}";
    }
}

var host = Host.CreateDefaultBuilder(args)
    .ConfigureServices(services =>
    {
        services.AddHttpClient();
        services.AddMcpServer()
            .WithStdioServerTransport()
            .WithTools<MyTools>()
            .WithPrompts<MyPrompts>();
    })
    .ConfigureLogging(logging =>
    {
        logging.ClearProviders();
        logging.AddSimpleConsole(cfg => cfg.SingleLine = true);
        // OR write to file, not stdout
    })
    .Build();

await host.RunAsync();
```

---

data.content:
**Project structure:**

```
├── Program.cs                 ← Host setup, DI, stdio transport
├── Tools/ExternalApiTool.cs   ← "fetch_url" tool (HttpClient via DI)
├── Prompts/SummarizationPrompt.cs ← "summarize" prompt template
└── McpServer.csproj
```

**Key design decisions:**

| Concern | Approach |
|---|---|
| **Stdio safety** | `LogToStandardErrorThreshold = LogLevel.Trace` — all log output goes to **stderr**, keeping stdout exclusively for JSON-RPC |
| **HttpClient** | `AddHttpClient()` registers `IHttpClientFactory`; the SDK auto-injects `HttpClient` into tool method params via DI (not included in the tool's JSON schema) |
| **Tool** | `fetch_url` — takes a `url` string, returns the response body |
| **Prompt** | `summarize` — takes `content` + optional `style` ("brief", "detailed", etc.), returns an instruction + content string |

Run with: `dotnet run` (or configure as an MCP stdio server in your client's config).
